author: @Tore_Lund
text: The classic Mini has now grown to the size of the classic Land Rover. The "old" Mini has a more manageble $ize for EV conversion.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I really don't like the new style mini

replies.author: @weegaz22
replies.text: The only thing about old Mini's is the chassis just wasn't designed for carrying a lot of weight like batteries if you wanted decent range, that and their rust magnets like typical British cars of the era, and extra weight kills the fun of really small cars, so the newer more modern mini is probably a better base to start from.

replies.author: @Tore_Lund
replies.text: @weegaz22 The official E-mini uses the same hardware as the BMW i3 and looking at the specs, they have cranked up the max current to give it the same acceleration as the i3 and at the same time it has shorter range on the same batterypack. Bjørn Nyland has done some HW tests in the e-Mini and highspeed consumption is high! So this is a heavy vehicle, with midiocre aerodynamics. There is an old Mini conversion done in the UK hovering around on Youtube, and it has reasonable performance with a small motor. I drive myself a 1800lb EV and I get 100mi on a 20 kWh battery pack that weighs only 250lb, so everything becomes cheaper and every household socket becomes a fastcharger. A classic Mini is smaller than my FIAT 600, so on a 20kWh battery and an efficient PM motor, it should get 170mi on a charge that takes 6 hr with a cable out your kitchen window.

replies.author: @weegaz22
replies.text: @Tore_Lund I suppose it depends on what you want from the vehicle, If i had a classic mini I wouldn't EV swap it, I'd rather drop a B16 Honda lump into it, keep it as a proper drivers car than a cheap city EV runabout.

replies.author: @Tore_Lund
replies.text: @weegaz22 Yes, that's fair but you can go cheap and Long Range with a small vehicle, without needing 65kWh of batteries. I like to holliday in my car and getting an EV in my price range, poses an obstacle, and that holds true for most conversions too, unless you throw money at a classic car conversion specialist who transplants the whole innards of a model S. So I get more Chargers to get sub one hour recharge time,  which means more frequent stops, but the same time spend on charging as with a big battery. Also getting a used big battery pack of good quality, is not exactly straight forward, unless you pay premium to a battery broker, or buy LiFePO4 cells from China + taxes and a good BMS which is also expensive. So all that, go for as small vehicle as possible when doing a conversion or expect to pay far more than you imagine.

author: @martinjandijkstra3205
text: Did you consider in-wheel electric motors? Those would make a conversion easier as there is no need to mount engines in the car.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes I did mate - it's likely to be what we go with

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: in wheel motors are great in many respects. But one should be aware that there are cons with them too,  mainly the inwheel motor's add unsprung weight which can  negatively effect handling.

replies.author: @martinjandijkstra3205
replies.text: @kreynolds1123 yes , I have read about that. Some advanced design uses internal springs. I dont know wether this issue is a big deal at all. I mean on modern roads with reasonable speeds..... Personally I wouldnt mind a small trade off in handling to get the conversion job done. Lets wait and see how the project evolves.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
@martinjandijkstra3205 modern  roads 🤔 . I have to say, some places have some pretty bad potholes. But yes driving slower and on well kept roads will handle great 😁.  Otherwise, handling will be a trade off. If the handling is tolerable (very subjective) for the conditions one will encounter for the majority of their driving, then inwheel electric motors will be welcome. 

stiffer springs suporting the additional weight of the batteries should go a ways to keeping the tires on the road, thus mitigating the effect of additional unsprung weight with inwheel electric motors.

replies.author: @martinjandijkstra3205
